segments.s263-g273: سرت را is the direct object: سرت (your head) with را marking definiteness/object. چنان (so/thus) modifies دور (far), and مانم is first-person singular (I keep / I will keep) in a poetic/presentive sense, so the clause literally says 'I keep your head so far.' ز پای is an ablative (poetic ز = از) 'from the foot(s)/body', indicating separation — together: 'I will keep your head so far from (your) feet/body.'
segments.s263-g274: کزان (from which / from that) introduces the result; پس here means 'after that/thereafter.' به لشکر is a prepositional phrase 'to the army.' نیایدت is a negative verb form (نیاید = 'will not come/arrive') with the enclitic -ت referencing 'you' (your/for you), and رای (counsel/plan) is the semantic item that 'will not come' — word order is inverted (verb before رای) as is common in poetic/classical Persian: 'from that time onward your counsel will not come to the army.'
segments.s263-g209: نوشته is a poetic nominal/predicative ('it is written' / 'written'); مگر here is adversative ('but rather' / 'however'); بر سرم is a prepositional phrase 'upon my head' (بر = 'on', سرم = 'my head' — the lemma senses list doesn't include 'head' so the precise sense is irregular); دیگرست is the copular/adjectival predicate 'is different/other'. Together they state that what is written upon the speaker's head is different/other (i.e. fate is otherwise).
segments.s263-g210: زمانه is the subject/nominative meaning 'time' or 'fate/fortune' here; به دست is a prepositional phrase 'in the hand/under the control of' (به + دست); جهان‌داورست is a nominal predicate 'is the World's Judge' with the copula '-ست' attached. The clause says that the order of events (time/fate) lies in the hand of the World's Judge.
segments.s263-g211: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'); بودنی is a nominal/participial idea 'being/existence' ('if there were any being/ground'); بود is the past copula 'were/was' marking the counterfactual; دل را is the definite object/topic 'the heart' (را marks it); به غم is a prepositional phrase 'to/with grief' that signals the intended emotional object. This subordinate clause sets up a hypothetical in which grief would be appropriate.
segments.s263-g212: سزد‌گر is an archaic fused expression functioning like 'سزد، گر' — 'it would be fitting; and if' — here signalling the evaluative result of the previous if-clause; نداری is used elliptically to mean 'if you lack (such a reason)' (the provided senses list gives 'lack' rather than a 2nd-person verb form); نباشی is a negative subjunctive/imperative 'do not be' (2nd person); دژم is an adjective 'sullen/gloomy'. The clause issues the practical advice: if you have no cause, do not be sullen.
segments.s263-g213: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'; بشنید is the past tense 'he heard' (3rd person); گفتار is the noun 'speech/words' functioning as the object of hearing; پور دلیر is a name or epithet 'Pour-dilīr / the brave son' in apposition to the source of the speech. The verb precedes its object in this poetic order.
segments.s263-g214: میان بسته is a compound participial/vernacular phrase: بسته (bound/closed) modifies the action in relation to میان 'the middle' — together meaning 'closed into the middle' or 'thrust into the midst'; جنگ is the battlefield/ fight that is the setting; بر سان is an archaic simile-construction ('بر' + 'سان') meaning 'in the manner of' or 'like'; شیر is 'lion'. The whole phrase describes a fierce, lion-like entry into the center of battle.
segments.s263-g215: فرودآمد is a past-tense verb 'he descended' or 'came down'; از marks source 'from'; دیزه is 'the fortress' or 'outwork'; راهجوی ('wayfarer' / 'one seeking the road') is the noun applied to the person who descended — here in apposition following the verb and source. Word order (verb before its modifiers) is common in classical/poetic Persian.
segments.s263-g216: سپر داد is a simple past clause 'he gave a shield' (سپر = shield, داد = gave). The conjunction و links the next object: درع سیاوش بدوی should be read as 'armor to Sîyâvash' — درع = armor; سیاوش = the personal name; بدوی here is an archaic fused dative ('بد' + 'وی' = 'to him') used to mark the recipient. The clause therefore means he gave shield and armor to Siyâvash.
segments.s263-g49: This clause describes an immediate action. وز آنجا = 'from there' (classical/place connective), بزد اسب is a simple past verb + object ('he struck/spurred the horse'); و links to the next verb phrase; برگاشت روی is an older/poetic phrasing meaning 'he turned his face' (برگاشت here functions like 'turned' + روی 'face/toward'). Word order is Verb‑Object then Verb+Complement, typical of classical Persian narrative.
segments.s263-g50: به نزدیک گودرز شد پوی پوی is a locative + result clause: به نزدیک = 'toward/near', گودرز = proper name (object of the motion), شد = 'became / came to be' used here to indicate movement or change of state (i.e. 'he went / came to'), and the repeated adjective پوی پوی is a poetic reduplication meaning 'swiftly / in haste.'
segments.s263-g51: ستایش‌کنان is an adverbial participle meaning 'praising' or 'while praising'. پیش او شد uses پیش 'before/in the presence of' + او 'him' and شد (classical past auxiliary) meaning 'he came/appeared'. به درد here means 'into pain' or 'in sorrow' (a state complement describing his emotional condition). The clause thus sets manner (praising) and emotional state on arrival.
segments.s263-g52: هم این داستان سر به سر یاد کرد is a single verbal clause: هم 'also/then' links to the previous action; این داستان 'this story' is the object; سر به سر is an idiomatic reduplication meaning 'throughout/entirely' or 'from head to tail'; یاد کرد is a light-verb construction where یاد (remembrance/account) + کرد ('did') = 'recounted' or 'told'. Word order: adverb + object + manner phrase + verb.
segments.s263-g53: که‌ای here is a poetic vocative or relative 'O you who...' (classical 'که ای'), followed by titles: پهلوان 'champion' and جهاندار 'world‑ruler' and شاه 'king'. The sequence of nouns is appositive—addressing the addressee with honorifics.
segments.s263-g54: شناسای (active adjective/participle) = 'knowing/knower of'; هر کار = 'every deed/affair' (هر = every, کار = deed/affair); و links to the second complement; زیبای گاه = 'the beauty of the time/occasion' (زیبای is adjectival/genitive 'of beauty' modifying گاه). The phrase describes qualities of the addressed king.
segments.s263-g55: شگفتی همی‌بینم = 'I see a marvel' (شگفتی noun 'wonder', همی‌بینم present/frequentive 'I see/observe'); از تو یکی = 'one from you' meaning 'one (marvel) in you' or 'one thing about you'. The clause is a simple subject‑verb‑object structure with the prepositional phrase indicating source/possessor.
segments.s263-g56: وگر چند هستم به هوش اندکی is concessive: وگر 'but if' + چند 'many/several' (elliptical 'if there are many'), هستم here functions as 'I am' (exist/stand) and is followed by به هوش اندکی = 'of little understanding' (به marking the domain/state, هوش 'intellect', اندکی 'small'). Together: 'but if there are many, I am of small understanding.'
segments.s263-g225: بر آن باره is a prepositional/time phrase 'on that occasion' (بر = 'on/upon' + آن باره = 'that occasion'). خسروی is the subject 'the king' (an epithet/noun) and برنشست is a past-tense verb meaning 'sat up / took a seat' (compound of بر- + نشست, an archaic/poetic past form). Word order places the temporal phrase before the subject and verb.
segments.s263-g226: کمر بست is a fixed expression 'girded his waist / girded his loins' (کمر = 'waist/girdle', بست = past 'bound/tied'). The conjunction و links the next clause. بگرفت is a past tense 'he seized / took hold' and its object گرزش is 'his mace' (گرز + possessive -ش = 'his mace'). به دست is an adverbial phrase 'into/in his hand' (literally 'to/into hand').
segments.s263-g227: یکی here is an indefinite 'a certain / someone'. ترجمان را is the definite object ('interpreter' marked by را). ز لشکر is a source phrase 'from the army' (ز = 'from'), and بجست is the past verb 'he sought / searched out' (classical past form).
segments.s263-g228: که introduces a relative clause modifying 'translator' ('who/that'). گفتار ترکان is a genitive phrase 'the speech of the Turks' (گفتار = 'speech', ترکان = 'the Turks'). بداند is a present/subjunctive-form of دانستن ('to know') used here to mean 'knows', and درست is an adverb 'correctly / properly'.
segments.s263-g229: بیامد is the past 'he came'. به سان is a fixed simile 'like / as', so the phrase means 'he came like ...'. هژبر ژیان functions as a name/epithet used in the simile (here treated as a proper name/epithet).
segments.s263-g230: به کین سیاووش is 'with enmity/a grudge toward Siavash' (به = 'with/for', کین = 'vengeance/grudge', سیاووش = the name Siavash). بسته میان is a participial phrase 'bound/tied in the middle (i.e., in his breast/heart)' where بسته = 'bound/tied' and میان = 'middle / midst' (poetic for 'in his breast'). Together: 'with a grudge against Siavash bound in his heart.'
segments.s263-g231: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. بیژن is the subject (Bijan). به نزدیک هومان is a prepositional phrase 'to the vicinity/near Houman' (به = 'to', نزدیک = 'near', هومان = proper name). رسید is the past verb 'reached / arrived'.
segments.s263-g232: یکی is indefinite 'a certain / one' (here 'he saw a...'). آهنین is an adjective 'iron(‑) / of iron' modifying کوه 'mountain'. پوشیده is a past participle used predicatively 'covered / clad' (literally 'an iron-covered mountain'), and دید is the past 'he saw'. Word order places the indefinite + adjective before the noun and the participle following as predicate.
segments.s263-g217: بدو (to him) is the indirect object 'to him'; گفت‌گر here introduces reported speech — a poetic compound meaning roughly 'he said' or 'he said thus' (گفت + -گر). کارزارت is کارزار 'battle/war' with the enclitic -ت ('your') and هواست is هوا + ست, where هوا 'desire/passion' is the predicate and ست is the copula 'is'.
segments.s263-g218: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'; بر + خرد is an adverbial phrase 'in/for wisdom' (i.e. 'to a wise mind'); کام تو places 'your desire/wish' as the subject of the following nominal predicate; پادشاست is پادشاه + ست used substantively as 'is kingship/sovereignty' (meaning 'your desire is for kingship').
segments.s263-g219: بر این باره is a circumstantial phrase 'on this occasion' or 'for this matter' (بر = 'on/for', این = 'this', باره = 'occasion'); گامزن is a noun 'strider/steed' (here designating the mount or one who steps) and برنشین is an imperative or exhortation 'sit on it / mount'.
segments.s263-g220: که introduces the reason/result clause 'for/because/that'; زیر تو اندر نوردد زمین has inverted word order: زیر تو = 'beneath you', اندر (in/within) and نوردد (third-person present 'rolls/rolls over' or 'traverses') govern زمین 'the earth' as the subject — 'the earth will roll/move under you'.
segments.s263-g221: سلیحم is سلیح + -م 'my weapon'; همیدون is a proper name (the weapon's name) placed appositively; به کار آیدت is an idiom 'to come to use for you' = 'be useful to you', where آیدت is آید + -ت ('comes to you').
segments.s263-g222: چو is temporal 'when'; با اهرمن کارزار آیدت = 'a battle with Ahriman comes upon you' (با = 'with', اهرمن = the Proper Name Ahriman, کارزار = 'battle/struggle', آیدت = 'befalls/comes to you'). The clause gives the condition/time when the weapon will be useful.
segments.s263-g223: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; اسب پدر is a genitive 'the father's horse' (اسب = 'horse', پدر = 'father'); دید is the past tense 'he saw'; بر پای پیش is a locative phrase 'before his feet / at the front (of him)' with the preposition بر and پیش 'in front'.
segments.s263-g224: چو باد is a simile 'like the wind' (چو = 'like/when', باد = 'wind'); اندر آمد is a verbal phrase 'came in/descended'; ز بالای خویش literally 'from its/top/above its back' (ز = 'from', بالای = 'above/on top of', خویش + ش = reflexive 'its/his own'), so the whole image is 'it came down from its back like the wind.'
segments.s263-g1: خبر شد is a fixed verbal phrase meaning ‘it was reported / news arrived’ (خبر + شد, past). به بیژن marks the experiencer with the preposition به + proper name. که introduces the reported content (‘that …’). هومان is the reported subject; چو شیر is a simile (چو = ‘like/as’, شیر = ‘lion’) modifying هومان. Word order follows older poetic style: reporting clause then the content clause introduced by که.
segments.s263-g2: به پیش = ‘into the presence of / before’; نیای تو is a genitival phrase ‘your ancestor’ (نیای = ancestor, تو = your). آمد is simple past ‘came’. دلیر is an adjective (= ‘brave’) placed after the verb as a predicate/adverbial qualifier of how he came. The clause is a continuative complement of که.
segments.s263-g3: چو is the temporal conjunction ‘when’. بشنید is simple past ‘he heard’ (third-person). بیژن is the subject (postposed to the verb, a common poetic order). برآشفت is a compound past verb meaning ‘became agitated/angry’ (بر + آشفت) and سخت is an adverb/adjective here meaning ‘severely/very’, modifying the degree of his anger.
segments.s263-g4: به خشم آمد is an idiomatic copular + noun phrase meaning ‘came into anger’ = ‘became angry’. آن شیر‌پنجه is a nominal phrase ‘that lion‑clawed one’ (آن = that/the, شیر‌پنجه = figurative epithet). ز introduces an ablative/causal relation (‘from / because of’), here followed by بخت ‘fate/fortune’ meaning roughly ‘by fate’ or ‘because of fortune’. Note the verb آمد precedes the subject phrase آن شیرپنجه in the poetic word order.
segments.s263-g5: بفرمود is the polite/honorific past verb ‘he commanded/said’. تا introduces the subordinate clause expressing what was to be done (‘that/so that’). برنهادند is a past plural verb (بر + نهادند) meaning ‘they lifted/put upon’. زین is the direct object ‘this (saddle)’ (زین = زین, saddle/trappings). The clause is therefore a jussive/complement introduced by تا.
segments.s263-g6: بر = ‘on/upon’. آن = demonstrative ‘that’. پیل‌تن is a compound adjective ‘elephant‑bodied’ describing دیزه (دیزه = fort/fortress). دوربین functions postpositively, here as ‘far‑seeing / observation’ (i.e. an observation apparatus or tower) modifying or apposed to دیزه. The whole phrase is a locative/goal phrase: ‘upon that elephant‑bodied fort’s observation‑place’.
segments.s263-g7: بپوشید is past tense ‘he put on / donned’. رومی‌زره is a compound noun ‘Roman armor’ (type of mail/armor) and جنگ here qualifies it as war‑gear (جنگ = war/battle). را is the definite/object marker placing focus on what was donned. The verb takes the whole noun phrase as its object in normal SOV/poetic order.
segments.s263-g8: یکی is an indefinite subject ‘someone / a certain one’. تنگ here works adverbially ‘tightly/closely’. بربست is past tense ‘fastened / bound’. شبرنگ را is the object marked by را (شبرنگ is a named garment/cloth, here ‘the shabrang’). The clause therefore means ‘someone fastened the shabrang tightly’.
segments.s263-g25: که is the subordinating particle 'that' introducing this clause; او را is the clitic object pronoun with the definite/object marker را ('him' as direct object). به نیزه is a prepositional phrase 'onto the spear' (به = toward/into + نیزه = spear). برافراختی is the 2nd‑person singular past perfective/preterite form 'you raised/hoisted' (poetic register). Word order is somewhat marked for poetry, but the clause reads literally 'that him‑OBJ onto spear you‑raised'.
segments.s263-g26: چو is a comparative/simile particle 'as/like' (here 'as when'); the following phrase gives the image of the comparison. بر ... بر ساختی is composed of the preposition/particle بر 'on/upon' + object (بابزن مرغ) + ساختی 'you placed/made', with ساختی in the 2nd‑person past. Note the object (مرغ 'bird') appears without را — a common poetic omission. The overall sense: 'as when you place a bird upon the gate‑post/door‑knocker.'
segments.s263-g27: تو‌ای is a vocative 'you!' (poetic address). مهربان 'kind' and بسیار‌هوش 'very‑wise/keen‑witted' are epithets placed around the name/title باب; the sequence is vocative inversion typical of classical Persian (‘O X, adjective…’). Here باب is being addressed as a person/title (vocative).
segments.s263-g28: دو کتفم 'my two shoulders' is the object phrase (کتفم = کتف + -م 'my'). به درع سیاوش is a locative/goal phrase 'into/with Siavash's cuirass' (به = into/towards + درع = armor + سیاوش owner). بپوش is a 2nd‑person imperative/subjunctive formed with the present stem and the prefix ب‑ meaning 'put on/cover' — here an order or entreaty: 'cover (them)'.
segments.s263-g29: نشاید is an impersonal negative construction 'it is not fitting/should not be'. جز از من 'except by me' marks exclusivity ('no one but I'). که introduces the content clause 'that I ...'; سازم is 1st‑person present/subjunctive 'I arrange/prepare/make' and نبرد is its object 'battle/war'. The clause says roughly 'It should not be except that I prepare the battle.'
segments.s263-g30: بدان functions here as 'by that / with that' (به + دان fused in poetry) and is followed by تا introducing purpose/result 'so that'. برآرم is 1st‑person subjunctive 'I may raise/lift/bring forth'. ز مردیش = 'from his manhood/valor' (ز = from; مردیش = his manliness/heroism). گرد here is poetic and can denote 'circle/glory/turn' (a noun of result); together the phrase expresses intent: 'so that I may draw forth glory from his manhood.'
segments.s263-g31: بدو is the prepositional clitic 'to him' (introducing indirect speech). گفت is the reporting verb 'said' (past) — here used as part of the narrative 'he said to him'. گیو‌ای is the vocative of the proper name Giv (گیو + ای). پسر 'son' is another vocative/appellative; هوش دار is an idiomatic exhortation 'have sense/be wise' (هوش = intellect; دار here expresses possession/imperative 'have' — 'be mindful').
segments.s263-g32: به گفتار من 'to my speech / to my saying' = 'attend to what I say' (به = to; گفتار = speech; من = my). سر به سر گوش دار is an emphatic idiom: سر به سر 'completely/throughout' + گوش دار 'have ear / pay attention' (گوش = ear/attention; دار = have/hold). Together: 'listen attentively to my entire speech.'
segments.s263-g233: ز (poetic از) introduces cause or source: 'because of / by'. جوشن is a noun 'armor/ cuirass' governed by ز. همه دشت is a noun phrase 'the whole plain' (همه = entire) and روشن شده is a compound verbal adjective/past participle ('has become bright / was lit'); word order is causal phrase → subject → result-state.
segments.s263-g234: یکی پیل = 'an/one elephant' (indefinite). در + زیر form the locative phrase در زیر = 'underneath'. جوشن is the noun 'cuirass/armor' following that locative, and شده is the resultive/passive 'has become / is so' indicating the elephant is (found) beneath the armor; overall: 'an elephant was beneath a cuirass.'
segments.s263-g235: از آن پس is a temporal phrase 'after that' (از = 'from/of' in this idiom). بفرمود is the literary/honorific past 'he ordered/said' (بـ- prefixed form of فرمود). تا introduces a subordinate/purpose clause ('so that/that'), and ترجمان is the object of that subordinate clause ('the interpreter').
segments.s263-g236: یکی here = 'one/a certain' (indefinite). بانگ is 'a cry/ shout'. برزد is an archaic poetic verb meaning 'let out/utter' (here used with بانگ to mean 'to raise a cry'); بر is the prefix/preposition 'at/upon/toward' and آن بدگمان = 'that suspicious one' (آن = that; بدگمان = suspicious). The clause thus gives the commanded action: 'let the interpreter utter a cry toward that suspicious one.'
segments.s263-g237: که‌گر (= که گر) is the classical conditional 'if'. جنگ جویی is a noun + deverbal construction meaning 'seeking/looking for war' (جویی = pursuit/search). یکی بازگرد is an imperative/jussive sense 'let one (person) return/withdraw' (بازگرد = return). The clause as a whole is conditional: 'If you/anyone seeks battle, let one withdraw.'
segments.s263-g238: که at the start of this clause gives the reason/explanation 'for/that'. بیژن is the subject name. همی is the archaic continuous/adverbial particle (marks ongoing or emphatic present). با تو is the prepositional phrase 'with you'. جوید is the present-tense verb 'seeks/searches' and نبرد is the object 'battle'. Word order is somewhat literary but reads: 'for Bijan is seeking battle with you.'
segments.s263-g239: همی‌گوید is the literary present 'he says / he keeps saying' (همی marks habitual/present). ای is the vocative 'O', رزم‌دیده is a compound adjective 'battle‑seen / battle‑scarred', and سوار is the noun 'rider/horseman' addressed directly.
segments.s263-g240: چه is the interrogative 'what / why'. پویانی is an archaic form related to پویان and here functions as a present-tense/active idea 'are you seeking/moving' (literary usage). اسب is 'horse' (the object being acted upon or associated with the rider), and اندر این مرغزار is the locative phrase 'in this meadow'. Together: 'What are you (and your horse) seeking in this meadow?'
segments.s263-g113: This clause is a temporal/conditional opener: تو ('you') + تا + verb phrase. Here تا functions in the classical sense 'as soon as/when' introducing what follows. چنگ را باز کردی is a transitive past action (باز کردن = 'to open/bare') with را marking چنگ as the definite object; the whole means 'you bared (or opened) the claw for battle.' The preposition به + جنگ (‘into/for battle’) indicates the context/purpose of the action.
segments.s263-g114: فروماند is a past/resultive verbal/adjectival form meaning 'was left/was forsaken/ remained behind' and governs the following oblique phrase. از جنگ marks source/reason ('from the fight'), and چنگ پلنگ is a noun phrase 'the panther's claw' (چنگ = claw, پلنگ = panther). The clause states the result: the panther's claw remained/was left as a consequence of the fighting.
segments.s263-g115: ترا دادم is object (ترا = you) before the verb + past tense دادم ('I gave' or 'I grant'); classical Persian often places the enclitic object before the verb. این رزم specifies the object 'this battle' and هومان is a vocative/name inserted; کنون is an adverb 'now' that frames the time of the statement.
segments.s263-g116: مگر introduces an exception or counterfactual here ('unless' / 'if not that'). بخت نیکت is a possessive noun phrase 'your good fortune' (note נیکت contains the possessive -ت 'your'). بود is the copula 'was/would be' linking to the predicate رهنمون 'guide' — so literally 'unless your good fortune were (our) guide' or more idiomatically 'unless your good luck be your guide.'
segments.s263-g117: گر begins a conditional clause 'if'. این اهرمن را marks 'this Ahriman' as the definite object. به دست تو is an agentive/instrumental phrase 'into/into the hand of you' (i.e. 'if it comes into your hands'), and هوش ('intellect/wits') is the subject that will 'arise' in the next segment — the clause intentionally leaves the verb to the following line (classical syntactic suspension).
segments.s263-g118: برآید is a third-person verb meaning 'arise/come forth' (here 'your wits awaken' or 'it becomes possible'). به فرمان یزدان is an instrumental/agentive phrase 'by the command of God' (به = by/through), and بکوش is the second-person singular imperative 'strive' (be- prefix + کوش). The sequence urges: if your wits awaken when the demon is in your hand, then, by God's command, make the effort.
segments.s263-g119: به نام is the fixed prepositional phrase 'in the name of' (به + نام). جهاندار یزدان is a title/epithet 'the Lord (ruler) of the world, God', and ما is the possessive/pronoun 'our/we' attached after یزدان to indicate 'our God' (classical Persian often places the possessor after the noun).
segments.s263-g120: به پیروزی is a prepositional phrase 'for the victory' (به = for/toward). شاه و گردان ما coordinates 'the king and our troop/host'; گردان ما carries the possessive 'our,' and syntactically both elements are governed by به پیروزی (i.e. 'for the victory of the king and our forces').
segments.s263-g105: وگر is the conditional particle 'if'. بازداری here is a second‑person present/imperative/nominalized form meaning 'you prevent / your preventing'. مرا زین سخن is the object: م(ـرا) + را = 'me' as definite object, زین سخن = 'from this speech' (زین = 'this' in older Persian, سخن = 'speech'). Word order: conditional particle → verb/noun → object.
segments.s263-g106: بدان روی is an idiomatic locution 'by/with that face / on that account' (ب- as a prepositional/prefix + دان used in the sense 'consider/regard' or forming the phrase 'بدان' = 'by that'); روی here = 'way/on account'. کآهنگ splits into که + آهنگ: که introduces a subordinate clause 'that', آهنگ = 'inclination/intent, disposition' (classical meaning). هومان is the personal name Homan. مکن is the negative imperative 'do not (do)'. The clause thus means 'do not, by that means, incline Homan / set Homan's intent.'
segments.s263-g107: بنالم is first‑person sing. present/future of نالیدن used as 'I will complain / I complain'. من is the subject 'I'. از پهلوان here means 'about / concerning the hero' (از + noun). پیش شاه = 'before / in the presence of the king' ('پیش' = 'before, in front of', شاه = 'king'). Word order is verb → subject (explicit) → prepositional phrase(s).
segments.s263-g108: نخواهم is the negated first‑person form 'I will not / I do not want to' (here idiomatically 'I will not [gird myself]'). کمر = 'girdle / belt / to gird one's loins' used metonymically for preparing for action. زان سپس = 'from that / after that' (زان = 'from that, on account of that'; سپس = 'then, afterwards'). نه کلاه = 'nor a helmet' (نه = 'not / nor', کلاه = 'hat/helmet'). The clause lists two things the speaker will not put on after that.
segments.s263-g109: بخندید (خندید with a prefixed ب‑) is past tense 'laughed' (classical narrative past). گودرز is the subject (proper name). و = 'and'. زو is poetic contraction of ز + او = 'from him / because of it/him' indicating cause. شاد شد = 'became glad' (شاد = 'happy', شد = past auxiliary 'became'). The clause sequence is: action (laughed) → conjunctive 'and' → cause phrase → result (became glad).
segments.s263-g110: به سان = 'like / as'; یکی = 'one / a single'; سرو = 'cypress' (a conventional Persian image for a tall, graceful person); آزاد شد = 'became free / was freed' (آزاد = 'free', شد = 'became'). The phrase is a simile: 'he became glad/was set free as a lone cypress.'
segments.s263-g111: بدو is a dative/enclitic phrase 'to him / unto him'. گفت here functions as 'said / spoke' (classical past), though the provided lemma senses listed are conversational nouns — the intended use is verbal 'said', so treat it as the reporting verb. نیک اختر = 'the auspicious/good star' (نیک = auspicious/good, اختر = star). و بخت گیو = 'and the fortune (fate) of Giv' (بخت = fate/fortune, گیو = proper name). Word order: indirect object (بدو) → reporting verb → subjects.
segments.s263-g112: که introduces the content clause 'that ...'. فرزند = 'a child / son'. بیند is subjunctive/present of دیدن used here in the sense 'will see / will have' (classical narrative uses بیند to mean 'will come to see / will obtain'). همی is an emphatic particle 'indeed / actually / just'. چون = 'like / such as'. تو = 'you'. نیو is uncertain here (textual/archaic) — it can be read as a form related to 'نو' = 'new' or could be a proper name; context suggests 'a son like you', so the final word is likely an intensifier or dialectal variant; its precise sense is ambiguous in the supplied senses.
segments.s263-g153: چو is the classical conjunction 'when' (introducing a temporal clause). پاسخ چنین یافت is a compact past construction: پاسخ (object) + چنین (demonstrative/adverb 'such/so') + یافت (past of یاب 'to find') = 'found such an answer'. چاره نبود is the verbless/past negative existential: چاره 'remedy/solution' + نبود (past of بودن) = 'there was no remedy.'
segments.s263-g154: یکی here is an indefinite numeral 'one' / 'a certain' emphasizing a single attempt. با پسر نیز places the accompaniment ('with the son') and نیز 'also' marks that the son too was involved. بند is the direct object meaning a 'bond/device/measure' (poetic/archaic use), and آزمود is the past tense of آزماییدن 'to try/test', so بند آزمود = 'he tried a device/bond.' Word order follows classical Persian: quantifier/subject phrase + prepositional phrase + object + past verb.
segments.s263-g155: به + proper name (به گودرز) marks the indirect object 'to Goudarz.' گفت is the reporting verb 'said' (classical past). ای is the vocative particle 'O', and جهان‌پهلوان is a compound title 'world‑champion / mighty hero' used in direct address.
segments.s263-g156: This is the quoted direction: به جایی 'to the place' + که introduces a relative clause 'that/where' (جایی که پیکار خیزد = 'the place where battle arises'). خیزد is the present/subjunctive form of خاست/خیزیدن meaning 'to arise/begin' (classical usage). The final به جان is an idiomatic phrase meaning 'at the cost of life / risking life' or 'with one's life'—here emphatic adverbial.
segments.s263-g157: مرا here (written as م + را in the word list) is the dative/object pronoun 'to/for me' used in an existential construction. خود is emphatic 'myself'/'indeed'. شب و روز is a frozen adverbial 'night and day' (i.e., constantly). کارست is an archaic fused form of کار + است 'there is work' or 'it is my task'; پیش at the end means 'ahead'/'before (me)'. The clause thus expresses 'For me, indeed, night and day there is work ahead.'
segments.s263-g158: چرا is the interrogative 'why'. داد here is the verb 'give' (classical past/stem used with باید) and باید 'must / ought' gives the necessity—classical word order places داد before باید, producing the sense 'should give / must give'. مرا (m + را in the list) is again 'to me' (dative), جان خویش = 'one's own life' (خویش = 'one's own', with the possessive enclitic ش 'his/her' attached). The whole clause reads as a rhetorical question: 'Why must I give my life?'
segments.s263-g159: نه ... نه ... is the classical correlative negative 'neither ... nor ...'. فرزند باید literally 'child (is) necessary' with باید indicating 'need/necessity', so 'I need no children'; گنج و سپاه are coordinated nouns 'treasure and army/troops' as additional items negated by the same correlative structure.
segments.s263-g160: The negation continues: نه آزرم سالار — آزرم is treated as a proper name (or a noun here) and سالار 'commander/chief' either as its apposition or a separate item; و links فرمان 'command/decree' with شاه 'king' forming 'the king's command'. All items are given as things the speaker does not require.
segments.s263-g65: The phrase is a small clause where the participial modifier 'به دام آمده' (having come into the trap) precedes the noun 'گرگ' (wolf), followed by the finite verb 'برگاشتی' (you opened/released). In older/poetic Persian the relative sense is formed this way: 'the wolf that had entered the trap — you opened/released (it/him)'.
segments.s263-g66: 'ندانم' is the present/factual clause 'I do not know.' The subordinate fragment 'کز این خود چه پنداشتی' literally 'what from this yourself you thought' uses the archaic connector 'کز' = 'که + ز/از' introducing the object clause; 'خود' is emphatic 'yourself'; 'پنداشتی' is 2nd‑person past 'you thought/imaged.'
segments.s263-g67: 'تو دانی' = 'you know' (present). The fused conditional particle 'که‌گر' = 'if' introduces the condition; 'خون او بی‌درنگ' places 'his blood' and the adverb 'immediately' before the clause-final verb in the next segment (typical subordinate word order).
segments.s263-g68: 'بریزند' is a 3rd‑person plural subjunctive/optative used in the protasis ('if they pour/shed'); the main clause follows: 'پیران نیاید به جنگ' — 'the elders will not come to battle.' Here 'نیاید' is the negative present/subjunctive form functioning like a future or general result ('will not come'). 'به جنگ' is the prepositional phrase 'to/into battle.'
segments.s263-g69: 'مپندار' is the negative imperative 'do not suppose/think.' The following 'کاو' in context functions as the old fused conjunctive 'که او' ('that he'); because the provided senses list does not include this conjunctive use, it is treated as irregular here. 'کینه بیش آورد' is a past clause 'brought/increased (more) hatred' with 'آورد' the past 3rd‑person singular verb at the end.
segments.s263-g70: The verb phrase 'سپه را ... پیش آورد' = 'he brought/advanced the army.' 'سپه را' is the definite object (را marks the army as definite), 'بر این دشت' is the locative 'onto/against this plain' and 'پیش آورد' is the past tense 'brought forward/advanced.' The subject is implicit (the 'he' introduced by the previous clause).
segments.s263-g71: 'من اینک' = 'I now.' The phrase 'به خون چنگ را شسته‌ام' places the instrumental/means 'به خون' ('with/in blood') before the object 'چنگ را' (the claw/hand, marked definite by را), and ends with 'شسته‌ام' (present perfect 'I have washed' / 'I have bathed'). This is a completed action with present relevance. Note 'چنگ' here is figurative (claw/hand), not the musical 'harp.'
segments.s263-g72: 'همان جنگ او را کمر بسته‌ام' literally 'I have girded my waist for that same war of/against him.' 'همان' = 'the same/that very,' 'جنگ' = 'war,' 'او را' is the object phrase indicating 'against him' or 'his (war),' and 'کمر بسته‌ام' is a fixed expression meaning 'I have girded (my loins)' — i.e., 'I have made myself ready for battle.'
segments.s263-g169: برانگیخت is a past tense verb placed before its object (poetic inversion) and means 'roused' or 'sprang up'; the agent is not expressed here. اسب is the direct object 'horse/steed' and از میان سپاه is a prepositional phrase 'out of/from the middle of the host' specifying where the horse came from.
segments.s263-g170: که introduces a relative clause 'which/that'; آید is a present-form verb used here to describe the horse's movement 'comes' (narrative/relative sense). ز لشکر means 'from the ranks/army' and به آوردگاه marks the goal 'to the battlefield.'
segments.s263-g171: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'; از پیش گودرز is a prepositional phrase 'from/before Goudarz' (پیش here = 'in the presence of' / 'in front of Goudarz'). شد + ناپدید is a light verb construction meaning 'became vanished' → 'vanished/was gone.'
segments.s263-g172: دل گیو is a possessive construction 'Giv's heart' (noun + name). ز indicates cause 'by/from' and اندوه او 'his sorrow' (او refers back to Goudarz); بردمید is a past verb meaning 'carried off/borne away' used figuratively: the heart was carried away (overwhelmed) by grief.
segments.s263-g173: پشیمان شد is 'became regretful' (شد = past auxiliary/verb 'became'). از درد دل = 'because of the pain of the heart' (درد دل = heartache). خون گریست is a compact phrase 'he wept blood' (خون modifying گریست: he shed blood in his weeping).
segments.s263-g174: نگر is an imperative or hortative 'look/consider.' تا here introduces the content clause 'so that / to see' and links to چیست at the end: 'see what ... is.' غم و مهر فرزند is the noun phrase 'the grief and affection of the child/son,' and چیست is the copula-question 'what is.'
segments.s263-g175: یکی is an indefinite 'one' / 'someone.' بآسمان is a contracted prepositional phrase 'to the sky/heavens' (ب = به). برفرازید is a past/poetic verb 'soared up / lifted up' and سر ('head') is the thing lifted — the object placed after the verb in poetic word order.
segments.s263-g176: This cluster describes bodily condition: پر از خون = 'full of blood' (پر used adjectivally 'filled with blood', modifying the previously mentioned head); دل از درد = 'a heart from/with pain' (i.e., heart wracked by pain); خسته modifies جگر ('weary/exhausted liver'), where جگر is used poetically as the seat of feeling. The adjectives and prepositional phrases stack to paint the wounded state.
segments.s263-g241: کز introduces a subordinate/clausal idea ('that/if'); the clause then gives its content: افراسیاب (proper name) is the source, اندر + آیدت literally ‘comes within to you’ — here اندر is the poetic locative and آیدت (آید + ـت enclitic) means ‘befalls you’. بد is an adjective ‘evil/bad’ that functions as the thing that comes. Word order is typical older Persian: clause marker + source + locative + verb+enclitic + predicate adjective.
segments.s263-g242: ز introduces origin/agent here (‘from’); توران زمین is a genitival phrase ‘the land of Turan’ (زمین = ‘land’). بر تو is the prepositional phrase ‘upon you’, followed by نفرین (noun ‘a curse’) as the subject/thing and سزد (impersonal/present notion ‘it befits’ or ‘is fitting’) as the predicate. The clause literally: ‘From the land of Turan, a curse upon you is fitting.’
segments.s263-g243: به کینه functions adverbially (‘with/under hatred’); پی‌افگنده is a participial/adjectival form ‘set upon / pursued (by)’, so the phrase describes the person’s state. و links the next adjective; بدخوی is an adjectival compound ‘ill‑tempered’. The whole unit is a descriptive phrase modifying the implied subject (you).
segments.s263-g244: ز here introduces the standard comparative phrase ‘than’: ز ترکان = ‘than the Turks’. گنهکارتر is the comparative adjective ‘more sinful’. کس normally means ‘anyone / person’. توی in this line functions as the addressed subject (‘you’ in an archaic/clipped form), completing the predication ‘you are X than anyone of the Turks’ — the word order puts the comparative phrase before the personal reference for emphasis.
segments.s263-g245: عنان is the direct object ‘reins/bridle’. بازکش here is an imperative (pull back / release the reins) addressed to the rider; the provided lexical senses don’t reflect this verbal imperative, so its specific sense is idiomatic/imperative in context. ز این تگاور هیون is a descriptive prepositional phrase ‘from/of this tɡāvar (rider/warrior), a hiun (beast/savage)’ modifying what one should seize/pull — i.e. the reins of this fierce rider/creature.
segments.s263-g246: کت in the manuscript is a problematic form here (likely a poetic/archaic connector or misprint of که) and does not match the lexical senses given, so its sense is unclear. اکنون ‘now’ sets time; ز کینه ‘from hatred’ gives cause; بجوشید (third person) describes the result — blood ‘boiled/seethed’ (here: emotion-driven bloodshed or blood starting to flow). خون is the affected noun ‘blood’. The verb here reports the event that just occurred as a consequence.
segments.s263-g247: یکی is the numeral/indefinite ‘one/a certain’; برگزین is the imperative ‘choose!’. جایگاه نبرد is a noun phrase ‘place/spot of battle’ (جایگاه = place; نبرد = battle), functioning as the direct object of the imperative. The phrase as a whole is an instructive command: ‘Choose a battlefield.’
segments.s263-g248: به here marks direction/area (‘into/through’): به دشت و در و کوه ‘into/through plain and valley and mountain’. با من is the comitative prepositional phrase ‘with me’. بگرد is an imperative of گردیدن ‘go about/ride/roam’ so the clause literally commands ‘go/roam with me through plain, valley and mountain’ — verb after the prepositional phrase is normal in Persian imperatives.
segments.s263-g249: وگر is the conditional 'if' (introducing a hypothetical). در میان = 'in the midst of / among' with preposition (در) + noun (میان). دو رویه سپاه = 'the two رویه of the army'—رویه here means an aspect/side/flank of the army (poetic use). سپاه is the head noun 'army'.
segments.s263-g250: بگردی is a present/subjunctive/imperative-form (2nd‑person singular) of گشتن/گشت‌گرد — here 'you roam/turn about'. به لاف = 'with/for boast' (به marks the attitude or means). از پی = a compound idea 'in pursuit of / for the sake of' (از + پی together express motive). نام و جاه are coordinated objects 'name (fame) and rank/station'. Word order places the verb early and the motive/goal after it, typical of poetic Persian.
segments.s263-g251: کجا introduces a rhetorical question 'where'. دشمن و دوست are coordinated nouns 'enemy and friend'. بیند is 3rd‑person singular subjunctive/present of دیدن 'see' used here in the sense 'can/may see'. ترا is the object marked with the 2nd‑person suffix (ت) + object marker را (here written separately) meaning 'you' as the thing seen.
segments.s263-g252: دل 'the heart' (emotional centre) is the subject of برگزیند 'will choose/select' (3rd‑person singular subjunctive/future sense in this rhetorical question). اکنون 'now' modifies the time. ترا again is the object 'you' with the suffix + را. The order places the mental subject (دل) before the temporal adverb and the verb, producing 'Where now will the heart choose you?'.
segments.s263-g253: چو بشنید = 'when he heard' (چو = 'when'; بشنید = past 3rd‑person 'he heard'). هومان is the subject (proper name). بدو = 'to him' (prepositional dative). گفت is the past of گفتن 'he said' introducing direct speech. زه here is an interjection 'ah/alas/lo' that begins Homan's utterance.
segments.s263-g254: زره را is the direct object 'the armor' (را marks definiteness). به کینم is a prepositional/benefactive phrase 'for/to my کین'—here کینم functions as 'my vengeance/grudge' (کین + possessive -م). تو بستی گره is a clause 'you tied (past) a knot' (تو = 'you'; بستی = past 2nd‑person of بستن 'to bind/tie'; گره = 'knot'). The whole segment is Homan's accusation: you have bound the armor with/for my vengeance and tied the knot.
segments.s263-g255: ز یزدان سپاس = 'thanks from/to God' (ز here = poetic 'from' or 'by' and یزدان = 'God'; سپاس = 'thanks'). و بدویم پناه = 'and I ran to him for refuge' — بدویم is a 1st‑person (archaic/poetic) form of دویدن 'I ran/hurried' and پناه = 'refuge/protection'; و links the clauses.
segments.s263-g256: کت آورد = 'he brought a coat' (کت = 'coat', آورد = past 3rd‑person 'brought'). پیشم = 'before me / in my presence' (prepositional locative). بدین رزمگاه = 'to this battlefield' (بدین is the fused prepositional phrase 'به + این' = 'to/for this' in classical/poetic usage, and رزمگاه = 'battlefield'). Note the poem's compact syntax: the action (brought) is followed by locative complements.
segments.s263-g129: پیاده شد is a past-tense verbal phrase meaning ‘he dismounted’ (literally ‘became on-foot’); شد is the past of شدن used here as a full verb. از اسب is a prepositional phrase ‘from the horse’ (source), and روی زمین (‘on the ground’) indicates the place he put his feet — the clause is elliptical and compressed in epic style (verb + source + place).
segments.s263-g130: ببوسید is the past tense ‘he kissed’ (direct action). و links the next action. بر باب literally ‘upon/at the gate/door’; کرد آفرین is a light verb construction: کرد ‘made/did’ + آفرین (an exclamation) = ‘he uttered/praised “Afarin”’. In Persian, کرد + noun here means ‘expressed that noun’ (i.e. vocally produced the acclamation).
segments.s263-g131: بخواند is the classical past (3rd sg.) of خواندن used here in the sense ‘he called/summoned’ or ‘he called (someone) by a name’. آن زمان = ‘at that time’ (آن acting as ‘then’). گیو را is the direct object with the object marker را, and پهلوان is the predicative noun — ‘he called Giv (a) champion.’ Word order is somewhat inverted (verb before time phrase) as is common in poetic diction.
segments.s263-g132: سخن گفت is a compound meaning ‘spoke’ or ‘held speech’ (literally ‘said speech’). با او = ‘with him’. ز بهر is a poetic prepositional pairing meaning ‘for the sake of’/‘on behalf of’ (ز here a poetic/archaic preposition used with بهر), and جوان is the beneficiary/target ‘the young man.’ Together: ‘he spoke with him about/for the young man.’
segments.s263-g133: وز here is the fused form of و + ز (and + از) meaning ‘and from’ (a common poetic contraction); آن خسروانی = ‘those Khosrowian/royal (men)’. زره یاد کرد = ‘he recalled/remembered the armor’ (یاد کردن = to remember). The clause thus says he remembered the armor belonging to those royal people.
segments.s263-g134: کجا is the interrogative ‘where’. خواست is the simple past ‘he wanted’ (here used in the sense ‘did wish’). بیژن is the subject (Bijan). ز بهر نبرد = ‘for the sake of battle’ / ‘to fight’ (ز بهر = ‘for’, نبرد = ‘battle’). The word order (question word + verb + subject) reflects classical/poetic inversion.
segments.s263-g135: چنین داد = ‘thus gave’ (داد here = ‘gave’). پاسخ پدر را = ‘the reply to the father’ (پاسخ is the reply; پدر را marked as the indirect object/recipient by را). پسر is the subject ‘the son’. The order (object phrases before subject and verb earlier) is poetic but the relation is: the son gave this reply to his father.
segments.s263-g136: که‌ای introduces direct speech here (که + ای, classical vocative/slightly archaic) — ‘saying, “O …”’. پهلوان جهان ‘champion of the world’ (پهلوان = hero/champion, جهان = world). سر به سر is an idiomatic intensifier meaning ‘entire/throughout’ or ‘all and sundry’, so together the vocative addresses him as ‘O champion of the whole world!’ The phrase is poetic and vocative in tone.
segments.s263-g193: بدو گفت is an object+verb phrase meaning 'he said to him' (بدو = to him/dative; گفت here stands for 'said' though given lexeme senses are nominal). ما را is a preposed object with the accusative marker را ('us' as the affected party). چه داری is an interrogative clause 'what do you have / what is your matter', and به تنگ is a prepositional/adverbial phrase ('toward narrowness' = 'in a tight/harsh way, i.e. against us') that completes the question.
segments.s263-g194: همی is an emphatic adverb ('indeed/just') placed before the predicate; تیزی here means 'swiftness' (a quality shown by the addressee). آری functions as an emphatic particle ('indeed') rather than a simple yes, and به جای درنگ is a fixed prepositional phrase 'in place of delay' (به جای = 'instead of', درنگ = 'hesitation/delay'), showing what the swiftness replaces.
segments.s263-g195: This noun phrase is the subject: سیه مار ('black serpent'). چندان + دمد forms a degree expression: چندان dmd (dmd = 'appears / comes forth') meaning 'appears to such an extent' and روز جنگ ('the day of battle') gives the time. Word order is typical classical Persian: degree/verb follows the subject.
segments.s263-g196: که introduces a result/clause of extent ('so ... that'); از ژرف دریا is a from‑phrase ('from the deep sea') and برآید نهنگ is the verb + subject inversion common in poetry: برآید ('arises/appears') + نهنگ ('a whale') — literally 'that from the deep sea a whale arises.'
segments.s263-g197: درفشیدن ماه is a verbal‑noun phrase functioning as the subject ('the moon's pressing/shining' — a poetic expression for the moon's brightness). چندان بود is the copular construction 'was so (much)', i.e. it sets up a degree/result clause introduced by که in the next segment.
segments.s263-g198: که introduces the result: 'that the sun, shining (تابنده), was hidden.' خورشید تابنده is a noun + participle ('the shining/radiant sun') and پنهان بود is the past copula ('was hidden'). Word order is subject + predicate with the copula at the end.
segments.s263-g199: کنون ('now') is a temporal adverb, سوی هومان ('toward Hōmān') is a direction phrase, and شتابی همی is a hortative/imperative-like expression: شتابی (here read as a hortatory/imperative 'hasten!') followed by همی, the classical continuous/emphatic particle. In classical Persian poetry the present form + همی often functions as an urging or command.
segments.s263-g200: ز فرمان من ('from my command' = 'away from my order') is a prepositional phrase expressing source/separation; سر بتابی is a verblike clause meaning 'you turn your head' (سر = 'head' literally) where بتابی uses the sense 'turn/bend', so together it means 'turn away' or 'disobey'. The final همی again gives an emphatic/continuous tone; the overall sense is a prohibition or warning not to stray from the speaker's command.
segments.s263-g17: که is the relative conjunction 'that/who' introducing the clause. از تن (literally 'from the body') is the prepositional phrase marking the source; سرانشان is the direct object 'their heads' (here literal body‑parts) with the possessive suffix -شان. جدا is an adverb/adjective 'apart/removed'. کرده‌دید is an archaic past perfect / compound past form (literally 'had done' or 'you had done'), 2nd‑person plural in older Persian; it functions as the main verbal predicate 'had cut/separated'. Word order is slightly inverted for poetic effect (preposition + source before the object).
segments.s263-g18: بدان is the locative phrase 'to/into that' (poetic contraction of به + آن); رزمگه is the noun 'battlefield'. جمله 'all' follows and scopes over the (omitted) direct object; افگنده‌دید is another archaic compound past 'had thrown/placed' (2nd‑person plural), agreeing with the previous past action. The object pronoun is omitted, typical in Persian; the two past verbs form a sequence of completed actions.
segments.s263-g19: نشان 'sign' is the noun head; آنک is an archaic subordinating particle meaning 'that' and introduces the following clause. ترکی is the noun 'a Turk' (here an individual), بیامد is the past tense 'came', and دلیر is an adjective 'brave' placed after the noun as an epithet (postpositive adjective), a common poetic order.
segments.s263-g20: میان دلیران is a prepositional phrase 'among the brave' (میان = among/mid, دلیران = plural 'the brave'). به کردار is a simile/comparative construction 'in the manner/character (کردار) of', where به marks the manner (instrumental/simile function), and شیر is the comparison noun 'lion'. Together: 'in the bearing of a lion.'
segments.s263-g21: به پیش ... رفت is 'went forward / advanced to the front' (به پیش = to the front, رفت = went). نیا here functions as a proper name or nominal (the text uses it as the actor); the instrumental phrase نیزه به دست 'spear in hand' follows the verb and supplies the means/attitude — also a common post‑verbal adjunct in Persian. This whole unit describes the motion + manner.
segments.s263-g22: همی is a frequentative/continual particle 'kept / was constantly'; بر خروشید is the verb 'roared/cried out' (بر as an emphatic or verbal prefix + خروشید 'roared'). بر سان مست is a poetic simile 'as/like a drunk' (بر + سان = 'in the manner of', مست = 'drunk'); the clause as a whole means 'he kept crying out, roaring like one intoxicated.'
segments.s263-g23: چنان بد کز is an archaic/resultive phrase 'so (bad/ill) that' — چنان 'so', بد here adverbializing the sense of 'ill/poorly', and کز (archaic contraction of 'که از') introduces the result clause. این لشکر نامدار is 'this renowned army/host' (این = this, لشکر = army, نامدار = famed/renowned).
segments.s263-g24: سواری نبود is an existential negative 'there was no rider/horseman' (سواری = rider/horseman, نبود = 'was not'). از در کارزار is a locative/idiomatic phrase 'on/at the field of battle' (در کارزار = in the battlefield; the preceding از is part of the older locution here). The clause states the outcome: the famed host lacked any mounted warrior in the fight.
segments.s263-g137: مرا is the dative/accusative pronoun 'to me' fronted for emphasis, followed by a coordinated list (هوش و جان و جهان) that functions as the logical subject/topic. این یکیست is a predication meaning 'this is one' (یکی + است fused as یکیست, a common poetic form). Overall: 'For me, intellect, soul and the world are one.'
segments.s263-g138: به چشمم ('in my eye' = 'in my sight') uses به + noun+possessive (چشمم) to mark viewpoint. چنین is an adverb 'thus/so', and جان او خوار نیست is a negated predication: جان او (his life/soul) + خوار (despised/low) + نیست (is not). The clause therefore means 'in my eyes his life is not so despised/insignificant.'
segments.s263-g139: بدو گفت is an inverted order meaning 'said to him' (بدو = 'to him/unto him', گفت here functions as 'said'); گودرز is the speaker's name placed after the verb (poetic flexibility). کای مهربان is a vocative: کای 'O' + مهربان 'kind one'.
segments.s263-g140: جز این برد: جز این 'besides/except this' + برد (here a noun 'gain/benefit'). باید به وی بر گمان is a modal phrase: باید 'ought/must', به وی 'in/for him' (i.e. 'in your view/regard' addressed to the addressee), بر گمان 'in opinion/estimation'. Together: 'Apart from this he ought to have some other gain, in your judgment.'
segments.s263-g141: که introduces a subordinate/explanatory clause ('for'/'that'). هر چند together functions idiomatically as 'although' (literally 'however much/even though'). بیژن جوانست: جوانست is the archaic/poetic 3rd‑person copula 'is young' (جوان + ست). و نو adds 'and new/inexperienced.'
segments.s263-g142: به هر کار 'in every affair' (به + هر + کار), دارد is the present tense 'he has/possesses', and خرد پیشرو is a noun phrase 'foregoing/leading wisdom' — literally 'wisdom that goes before', i.e. foresight or prudent counsel. Word order (prepositional phrase → verb → noun phrase) is typical of Persian.
segments.s263-g143: و دیگر 'and moreover/another thing' introduces an additional point. که این جای کین جستن است is a finite clause: این جای 'this place', کین جستن 'seeking vengeance' (کین as noun 'vengeance' + جستن 'to seek'), and است 'is' — 'and furthermore, this is a place for seeking vengeance.'
segments.s263-g144: جهان را ز آهرمنان شستن است: جهان را (object-marked for emphasis) 'the world', ز آهرمنان 'from the Ahrimans' (i.e. the forces of the devil), شستن است is an impersonal-style infinitival predicate 'it is to wash/cleanse'. Together: 'it is to cleanse the world of Ahriman (his malign ones).' The object marker را highlights جهان as what is to be cleansed.
segments.s263-g185: به دل گفت = an indirect-object prepositional phrase (به + noun) placed before the verb, literally 'said to the heart.' خیره modifies the following verb as an adverb ('by staring' / 'with a fixed gaze'). بیازردمش is a 1st‑person verb form with the object clitic -ش ('him'); in classical/poetic Persian this form expresses a potential or result (literally 'I would have vexed/angered him' or 'I would have hurt him by staring').
segments.s263-g186: چرا is a rhetorical interrogative 'why.' خواسته here is a perfect/participial sense 'having wished' or 'when he desired.' پیش is 'before / in the presence of.' ناوردمش is the past form of ناوردن ('to bring') with the object clitic -ش, meaning 'I did not bring him (before me).' The clause as a whole asks why the speaker did not bring the person forward when he wished.
segments.s263-g187: گر is the conditional 'if.' او را uses the object marker را after the pronoun (emphatic/accusative). ز هومان = 'from Hūmān' (ز = poetic 'from', هومان a proper name). بد آید به سر is an idiom: آید (present/subjunctive 'come' / 'befall') with بد 'bad' and به سر 'to the head' → 'badness befalls (him)' / 'misfortune comes upon him.' Word order puts the conditional clause first.
segments.s263-g188: چه here is the interrogative 'what' ('what (is) ...?') introducing a rhetorical question. باید is the modal 'must / is necessary.' م (here printed separately) is not the object pronoun but a textual fragment in the segmentation; grammatically the object is مرا ('to me'), where را is the object marker attached — the sense expressed is 'what use (is) to me.' درع و تیغ و کمر are three nouns listed in the normal Persian noun-and-noun pattern ('armor and sword and belt'). The clause asks rhetorically what benefit those arms have for the speaker.
segments.s263-g189: بمانم is a 1st‑person present/subjunctive of ماندن ('I remain' / 'let me remain' / 'that I remain') used here to express the state the speaker imagines or laments. پر از is the standard adjective + preposition 'full of.' The nouns حسرت، درد، خشم are listed as objects of 'full of' (regret, sorrow/pain, anger). Word order places the verb before the predicative phrase, a common poetic feature.
segments.s263-g190: This continues the 'full of' pattern: پر از آرزو = 'full of longing/desire.' دل (heart) is the possessor and is then itself described: دل پر از آب چشم = 'a heart full of water (i.e. of tears).' آب چشم is a poetic compound where آب is literal 'water' used metaphorically for tears, followed by چشم 'eye.'
segments.s263-g191: وز آنجا is a poetic locative phrase 'from there' (وز is a variant/poetic form related to از/از آنجا). دمان (دامان) 'hem/skirt' is the subject; هم 'also' adds inclusion. به کردار گرد = 'in a circular manner' or 'with a circular motion/turn' (کردار here = 'manner/behavior' and گرد = 'around/round'). The sequence describes motion of the garment or the movement accompanying the actor.
segments.s263-g192: به پیش پسر شد = 'he moved/advanced before the youth' (به پیش = toward/in front of; پسر = the young man/son; شد = became/went — here a simple past motion 'he went'). به جای نبرد = 'to the place of battle' or 'to the spot for fighting' (به جای = to the place, نبرد = battle). The clause describes his movement forward to the battlefield in front of the youth.
segments.s263-g145: This small clause strings together two prepositional phrases that qualify the action to follow. "به کین سیاوش" uses به + noun to mean "out of / for (the) vengeance (toward) Siavash"; "سیاوش" is simply the proper name. "به فرمان شاه" uses a second به in its instrumental/agentive sense ("by / at the command of") plus فرمان ("command") and شاه ("king"). The order is poetic: qualifiers precede the main predicate which will follow.
segments.s263-g146: نشاید is an impersonal present (or modal) predicate meaning "it is not fitting / one should not." It governs an infinitival/nominal clause introduced by به + verbal-noun: "به پیوند کردن" (to make a bond/link) where پیوند is the noun "bond/link" and کردن is the light verb "to make/do." نگاه here is a noun meaning "regard/consideration/attempt" (not a literal gaze) and pairs with به to mean "to think of / set about" doing the action.
segments.s263-g147: و‌گر is the conditional "and if / but if." بارد here is a poetic form meaning "falls/rains" (from باریدن) — this sense is not among the supplied options, so it is marked as unmatched. The following از ابر پولاد تیغ strings the image: از ("from") + ابر ("cloud") + پولاد ("steel") + تیغ ("blade/sword"), forming a poetic "blade of steel falling from the cloud."
segments.s263-g148: نشاید again gives the impersonal "it is not fitting." که introduces the content clause that follows. داریم ما is the present-tense first-person plural "we have/possess" with ما emphatic. جان دریغ is the object: جان = "life," دریغ = "reluctance / holding back," so the clause says it would not be fitting that we hold back our lives (i.e., spare them) out of reluctance.
segments.s263-g149: نشاید introduces another negative/unsuitability judgment. شکستن دلش را is a verbal-noun phrase where شکستن (breaking) takes دلش را ("his heart" with the possessive ش and the object marker را) as object. به جنگ attaches as a locative/instrumental phrase "in/through war" describing how the heart would be broken.
segments.s263-g150: بپوشیدنش is the verbal-noun + enclitic pronoun meaning "to clothe him / to put (it) on him." جامه is the noun "garment," and نام و ننگ is a fixed pairing meaning "name/reputation and shame/infamy." Grammatically this continues the unsuitability judgment: it is not fitting to clothe him in such a garment of (so-called) reputation and disgrace.
segments.s263-g151: که here introduces an explanatory clause "for/that / when." چون کاهلی پیشه گیرد is a common classical construction: چون = "when/as," کاهلی = "laziness/idleness," پیشه گرفتن = "to adopt/take up as a practice." گیرد is a present subjunctive/ordinary form "takes up/adopts." جوان is the subject here meaning "youth / the young (person)."
segments.s263-g152: بماند is the present "remains / is left." منش = "character" and پست = "low / base," so منش پست = "base character." و تیره روان links another predication: تیره روان = "dark/bleak of spirit/soul." The clause describes the moral effect: persistent idleness leaves one with a base character and a gloomy spirit.
segments.s263-g257: به لشکر: a prepositional phrase 'to the army' (به = to/for + لشکر = army). بر آن سان: an adverbial phrase 'in that manner/so' where بر is a prepositional/relational particle and آن سان = 'that way' (poetic order). فرستمت باز: the verb فرستمت is 1sg past tense with the object clitic -ت ('I sent you'); باز after the verb functions adverbially 'back/again'. Overall word order: prepositional phrase + manner phrase, then verb with object clitic and adverb.
segments.s263-g258: که introduces a subordinate/result clause 'so that'. گیو is the subject (proper name). از تو ماند is literal 'remained from you' — here meaning 'remains with you' or 'is left in your care' (ماند is past/stative 'remain, be left'). به گرم و گداز is a predicate/state phrase 'in heat and melting' (poetic imagery: ardor/anguish); به here marks the state/condition.
segments.s263-g259: سرت را is the definite direct object ('your head' + object marker را). ز تن دور مانم is literally 'I keep [it] distant from the body' — مانم is 1sg present/subjunctive of ماندن used here with a causative/continuative sense 'I will keep/leave'. نه دیر 'not long' negates the duration (short time). Word order places the object before the verb, typical of Persian.
segments.s263-g260: چنان کز = 'such that/from which' (کز = که + از). تبارت is 'your lineage' (تبار + enclitic ت). فراوان دلیر is an adjectival/nominal phrase 'abundant (are) brave ones' — the verb is omitted (typical in poetry) and understood: 'so that from your stock many brave men (will be) abundant.'
segments.s263-g261: چه سودست is an idiomatic question 'what use/benefit is it' where سودست is a contracted poetic form (= سود است). کآمد is an enclitic fused relative/verb 'that/it came' (کآمد = که آمد). به نزدیک شب means 'near night' or 'night has come near' — به marks direction/state.
segments.s263-g262: رو here is an archaic/poetic imperative 'go/turn' or 'face' used as an exhortation. اکنون 'now' is an adverb. به زنهار is an idiom 'to the warning' = 'take heed/beware', where زنهار can function as imperative/verbal admonition. تاریک شب 'dark night' is the object/phrase being warned against. The clause is an admonition: 'Go now — beware the dark night.'
segments.s263-g263: من اکنون emphasizes the subject 'I' and time 'now'. یکی باز is 'one/once again' (یکی as an indefinite/one, باز = again). لشگر شوم uses شوم (1sg subjunctive of شدن) 'I become' — here a volitional/future sense: 'I will become (an) army' (poetic hyperbole: I myself will form an army). Word order: subject + temporal + modifiers + predicate.
segments.s263-g264: به شبگیر نزدیک is a locative/relational phrase 'near the night‑visitor/night‑raider' (به marks orientation, شبگیر = one active at night, نزدیک = near). مهتر شوم repeats the verb شوم 'I become' with مهتر 'chief' as the predicative noun: 'I will become chief (by/near the night‑raider)'. Again شوم carries a future/intentive sense.
segments.s263-g121: بگویم is an archaic/hortatory-form verb meaning 'I will say' or 'let me say' (first-person subjunctive/imperative flavor). کنون 'now' follows it as a temporal adverb. گیو را is the definite/direct-object phrase ('Giv' + object marker را), and کان زره is a noun phrase modifying that object: کان here functions as a demonstrative/relative-like word pointing to 'that/the said' armor, followed by زره 'armor'.
segments.s263-g122: که begins a subordinate/relative clause ('that' / 'which'). بیژن is the subject. همی‌خواهد combines the archaic prefix همی (continuous/presentive) with خواهد 'wants' to mean 'wishes' or 'is minded to'. او را is the object pronoun 'him' (او) + object marker را. بده is the verb 'give' in an older/clipped form (here functioning as the subordinate verb: 'to give' / 'that he give him').
segments.s263-g123: گر is the conditional 'if'. ایدنک is an archaic verb meaning 'to come / to arrive' (here 'should come' / 'should arrive'); combined with پیروز باشی ('be victorious') it yields a protasis: 'if you come/should come victorious'. بر اوی is a prepositional phrase 'against him' (بر + او with final -ی), so the whole segment means 'if you should come victorious against him.'
segments.s263-g124: ت + را together mark the 2nd-person object 'you' (here written as separate ت followed by the object marker را = 'ترا'). بیشتر is the comparative 'more'. نزد من is a prepositional phrase 'in my presence / before me'. آبروی is the head noun 'reputation / honor' and stands at the end as the predicate: 'you will have more honor in my sight.'
segments.s263-g125: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (used to indicate 'above' in the comparison). فرهاد و گیوت are the two names 'Farhad and Giv' (گیوت = گیو + ت). برآرم is first-person present/subjunctive 'I will raise / I will set above' (بر + آرم). به جاه indicates the realm/result 'in rank/status' to which the raising applies.
segments.s263-g126: This is a series of prepositional phrases giving the domains in which the addressee will be elevated: به گنج و سپاه ('in treasure and army') و به تخت و کلاه ('and in throne and crown/helmet'). The repeated به introduces parallel complements; کلاه here functions as headgear (crown/helmet) rather than a literal 'hat.'
segments.s263-g127: بگفت is simple past 'he said' or 'he spoke'. این سخن 'this speech / these words' is the object. با here functions as 'to/with' introducing the person addressed; نبیره نیا is a noun phrase 'the scion/descendant of Nia' (نبیره = scion/offspring, نیا treated as a name or ancestral epithet). The overall structure: 'He said these words to Nia's scion.'
segments.s263-g128: This is an appositive description: نبیره (repeating the noun) + پر از 'full of' + بند و پر کیمیا. پر از is the fixed phrase 'full of', بند here most likely means 'bonds / ties' or possibly 'stanzas' in poetic contexts (the sense is ambiguous), and کیمیا literally 'alchemy' but often used metaphorically for secret art or wondrous quality. Grammatically, the second نبیره is in apposition and the two 'پر' phrases parallel each other.
segments.s263-g81: ز شادی ('from joy') is a prepositional phrase giving the cause of the following verb. بر او آفرین کردن is a fixed verbal phrase meaning 'to acclaim/praise someone' where بر او is the preverbal indirect object and آفرین ('bravo') is a nominal exclamation used as the object; کرد is the past tense auxiliary 'did' and سخت is an adverbial 'strongly/earnestly' modifying کرد.
segments.s263-g82: که introduces a reported clause (here a wish/benediction). The clause is poetically compressed: از تو ... جاوید بخت means 'may eternal fortune not depart from you' (object جاوید بخت 'eternal fortune' comes after the negated/ambiguous verb). مگرداد is an archaic/ambiguous item (see note) and the word order (object before verb) is typical poetic inversion.
segments.s263-g83: تو تا introduces a temporal clause: تا here functions as 'when / as soon as'. برنشستی is the past tense 2nd-person form composed of the prefix بر- + نشستی 'you sat/mounted' and takes the following locative phrase به زین پلنگ ('on the panther-saddle'), where زین is the saddle and پلنگ is a poetic epithet ('panther'/'leopard').
segments.s263-g84: This is a compact coordinated clause. نهنگ is used as a nominal subject (here likely an epithet or name) and از دم آسود literally 'was eased from breath' = 'sighed / was relieved'; آسود is a predicative participle 'at rest/relieved'. The conjunction و links the second phrase, where شیران ز جنگ is elliptical (the verb آسود is understood to apply to 'the lions' as well): 'and the lions were freed from battle.'
segments.s263-g85: به هر کارزار اندر آیی is an optative/jussive construction expressing a wish: آیی ('you come/enter') is used in the sense 'may you enter' rather than a simple statement of fact. اندر is an older/prepositional form meaning 'in/into'. دلیر follows as a predicative/adjectival complement 'brave.'
segments.s263-g86: This second clause is parallel and also optative: پیروز باشی uses باشی in the subjunctive/optative sense 'may you be' and پیروز 'victorious' is its predicate. چو شیر is a simile 'like a lion.' Word order places the predicate before the subjunctive auxiliary, a common poetic arrangement.
segments.s263-g87: نگه کن is an imperative 'watch / take heed' (نگه nominal + کن imperative 'do'). که introduces the content clause giving the reason/condition. با او به آوردگاه is a prepositional phrase 'with him to the battlefield' (با او marks accompaniment; آوردگاه = 'battlefield').
segments.s263-g88: توانی شدن is a periphrastic ability construction: توانی ('you can') + شدن ('to happen / to make happen') here yields 'if you can cause him to come' or 'if you can bring it about'. زان پس ('from that, thereafter') sets the temporal consequence; آورد is 'bring' (root meaning 'bring/carry'), and خواه at the end is a modal/volitional particle 'if you will / if you wish' (literally 'want'), making the whole clause conditional/polite: 'if you can bring him into the fight, then afterwards you may bring him, if you wish.'
segments.s263-g177: به دادار گفت = 'he said to the Creator': به marks the indirect object, دادار is the addressee ('the Creator') and گفت is the past tense verb 'said'. Immediately after, ار introduces a conditional/subjunctive clause ('if' / 'should it be that') and جهان‌داوری functions as a vocative or epithet ('O Judge of the World' — an address to God). The whole segment sets up direct speech introduced by 'he said' + conditional 'if'.
segments.s263-g178: یکی سوی این خسته‌دل بنگری = 'one / if one looks toward this weary‑hearted (one)': یکی here is an indefinite 'one' or 'someone', سوی = 'toward', این = 'this', خسته‌دل is a compound adjective/noun meaning 'weary‑hearted' (lovesick, sorrowful), and بنگری is the subjunctive/optative form of نگریستن (ب‑ + نگرِی stem) meaning 'might look' or 'would look'. The verb in subjunctive completes the conditional begun by ار.
segments.s263-g179: نسوزی تو از جان بیژن دلم: نسوزی is used as a stative/experiential 'is burning/aching' (here expressing grief); تو is the vocative/emphatic 'you' (addressing Bijan), از marks the cause/source ('for' or 'because of'), جان بیژن = 'Bijan's life', and دلم ('my heart') at the end names the experiencer/topic ('my heart (is) ...'). Word order is poetic (verb + vocative + cause + topic).
segments.s263-g180: که ز آب مژه تا دل اندر گلم: که introduces an explanatory clause ('for/that'), ز is the poetic form of 'from', آب مژه literally 'water of (the) eyelash' = 'tears' (آب = water/tears, مژه = eyelash), تا = 'as far as'/'up to', دل = 'heart', اندر = 'in/within', and گلم (گل + ـم) is a possessed noun 'my گل' (here poetically imageable as 'my flower' or 'my clay/earth' depending on context). The clause describes extent/source of feeling: 'from my tear‑water up into/within my heart.'
segments.s263-g181: به من باز بخشش تو‌ای کردگار = 'to me again (a) forgiveness, O Creator': به من marks the beneficiary ('to me'), باز = 'again', بخشش is the noun 'forgiveness' or 'pardon' (object requested), and تو‌ای کردگار is a poetic vocative 'you — O Maker/Creator' addressing God. The clause is a petition framed before the imperative that follows.
segments.s263-g182: بگردان ز جانش بد روزگار = 'turn (away) bad fortune from his life': بگردان is an imperative 'turn!' (here 'remove' or 'turn away'), ز = 'from', جانش = 'his life' (جان + possessive suffix ش), بد modifies روزگار meaning 'bad/ill', and روزگار = 'time/fate/fortune' — together: 'ward off ill‑fortune from his life.'
segments.s263-g183: بیامد پراندیشه دل پهلوان: بیامد is the past tense 'came', پراندیشه is a prefixed adjective 'full of thought' / 'pensive' modifying the following noun, and دل پهلوان (poetically without an explicit ezāfe) means 'the champion with a thoughtful heart' or 'the hero, whose heart was full of thought' — subject and its descriptive phrase come after the verb in this poetic order.
segments.s263-g184: پر از خون دل از بهر رفته جوان: پر از = 'full of', خون دل = 'heart‑blood' (blood of the heart, i.e. intense sorrow or wound), از بهر = 'for the sake of' / 'because of', and رفته جوان = 'the young (man) who has gone/departed' (رفته is a past participle modifying جوان). The phrase describes the hero's inner state: his heart filled with blood (grief) for the departed youth.
segments.s263-g89: که is the subordinating conjunction 'that' linking back to the previous clause. هومان is the proper name/subject. یکی here means 'a certain' (indefinite article-like). بدکنش is a compound noun 'bad-doer / ill-disposed one' qualifying Homan. ریمن is a place or ethnonym attached to بدکنش (’the ill-doer of Rimen’). است is the copula 'is'. Word order is typical Persian: conjunction + NP + copula.
segments.s263-g90: به آورد is a verb phrase where به + آورد conveys 'brings about / causes' or 'brings (into)'; آورد is a finite verb here. و is the coordinating 'and'. جنگ او = 'his war / his fighting' (جنگ with the possessive clitic -ش understood). چو = 'like / as'. آهرمن (Ahriman) is a simile target, and است is the copula 'is'. The clause states that he brings/causes war and that his fighting is like Ahriman.
segments.s263-g91: جوانی 'youth' and ناگشته 'untried / inexperienced' form a nominal phrase joined by و. بر سر سپهر is a locative expression literally 'upon the head of the sky', i.e. 'under the heavens'; بر + سر + سپهر is a fixed poetic locution meaning 'under the sky'.
segments.s263-g92: نداری here functions as a second‑person negative 'you do not have' (negative prefix ن- plus داری), expressing lack. همی is the archaic/progressive particle often marking continuous/present aspect. بر تن خویش مهر is the phrase 'a mark/affection on (one's) body': بر = 'on', تن = 'body', خوی + ش = 'one's own' (خویش, reflexive/possessive formed with the clitic ش), مهر = 'love/affection' (here 'a mark/ token of affection'). The overall meaning: you do not yet bear that mark upon your body.
segments.s263-g93: بمان is an imperative 'stay / remain'. تا introduces the subordinate temporal clause 'until'. یکی here is indefinite 'a certain / one (someone)'. رزم دیده is a compound adjective 'battle-seen' = 'battle‑experienced / veteran' (رزم 'battle' + دیده 'seen' as past participle). هژبر is a personal name, the noun head of the phrase. The whole NP means 'a certain battle‑tested Hežbar.'
segments.s263-g94: فرستم is the speaker's verb 'I will send / I send' (first‑person finite, here prospective/intentional). به جنگش = 'to/against his battle' i.e. 'against him in battle' (جنگ + possessive clitic -ش). به کردار ابر is an adverbial phrase 'in the manner/guise of a cloud' (به کردار = 'in the manner of', کردار here 'manner/way', ابر = 'cloud'), specifying how the one sent will fight or come. Word order places the verb before the adverbial phrase.
segments.s263-g95: بر او = 'upon him' (بر + object pronoun او). تیرباران is a compound noun/verbal noun 'arrow-rain' meaning 'a showering of arrows'. کند is the third‑person singular present/habitual 'he does / will do' (here describing what the sent warrior will do). چون تگرگ is a simile 'like hail' modifying the verb phrase. The clause says he will pelt him with arrows as if hail were falling.
segments.s263-g96: به سر بر = locative 'on the head' (به + سر + the prepositional بر used for emphasis/location). بدوزدش is the verb 'he sews / will sew' with the enclitic object -ش 'it/him/her' attached (so 'he sews it onto him'). پولاد ترگ is the object 'a steel (پولاد) shred/rag (ترگ)' — a compound nominal where پولاد modifies ترگ. The clause describes fastening/stitching a piece of steel onto the head (a vivid, possibly hyperbolic image).
segments.s263-g265: وزآنجا is an adverbial phrase 'and/from there' placed at the clause start. دمان گردن افراخته is a nominal/adverbial phrase describing manner: دمان (a poetic 'skirt/hem' or 'edge', used here as a locative/adverbial) + گردن افراخته ('neck held high', a noun + past participle adjective) — together indicating coming with head held high/proudly.
segments.s263-g266: بیایم is the 1st-person present-subjunctive used here with future/intentive force ('I will come' / 'I come'). نبرد ترا ساخته is a small predicative unit: نبرد ('battle') as the noun-object, ترا+را the 2nd-person object ('you'), and ساخته the past participle 'made/prepared' used predicatively — 'having made/prepared battle against you.' Word order places the verb-like element (بیایم) before this object phrase.
segments.s263-g267: چنین پاسخ آورد is a conventional narrative formula 'gave such an answer / replied thus'; آورد here is the past tense of آوردن used as 'said/reported' (i.e., relate/report). که introduces the reported speech, followed immediately by شو — an imperative/subjunctive 'be' directed at the addressee.
segments.s263-g268: پست باد is an optative/formulaic curse: پست (an insulting adjective 'low, base') + باد (optative particle 'may / would that') = 'may you be low/base.' The conjunction و links a second wish: آهرمنت پیشرو = 'Ahriman (proper name) as forerunner' (پیشرو 'forerunner' or 'vanguard').
segments.s263-g269: همه دشمنان 'all the enemies' is fronted; سر به سر is an idiomatic phrase meaning 'one and all' or 'one after another' (here 'one and all'). کشته is a past participle 'slain,' and باد is the optative particle 'may' — together forming a wish: 'may all the enemies be slain.'
segments.s263-g270: گر is the conditional 'if.' آواره از جنگ برگشته is a nominal clause 'one who has been straying/wandering and has returned from the war' (آواره used as a noun/adjective + از جنگ برگشته 'returned from the war'). باد again is the optative particle, so the whole conditional clause carries the same wished fate (understood from the previous clause): 'if a wanderer returns from the war, may he be (slain) as well.'
segments.s263-g271: چو 'when' introduces a temporal clause. فردا 'tomorrow' is the temporal adverb, and بیایی is the 2nd-person present-subjunctive ('you come') used for a future event. به آوردگاه is the prepositional phrase 'to the battlefield' indicating destination.
segments.s263-g272: نبیند is the 3rd-person negative present-subjunctive/optative form 'may not / will not see'; ترا+را is the 2nd-person object 'you' marked by the object particle را. نیز here functions as 'also/likewise' (in this negative context it contributes the sense 'nor/also not'), and شاه و سپاه (king and army) are the postponed subjects at the end — Persian commonly places the verb before the subject.
segments.s263-g161: اگر introduces a conditional ('if'). جنگ جوید is an older-style present/ subjunctive clause meaning 'seeks battle' (جوید is 3rd person present/subjunctive of جستن/جوییدن). سلیحش is سلیح + enclitic -ش ('his weapon'); کجاست is the interrogative 'where is it?' (کجا + است). The clause thus reads literally 'If he seeks battle, his weapon where-is?'
segments.s263-g162: زره دارد = 'he has armor' (دارد = present 'has'). از من is a prepositional phrase 'from me / of me'. چه بایدش خواست is a rhetorical question: باید (modal 'ought/should') + ش (enclitic pronoun referring back to 'armor' or 'it') + خواست (here functioning as 'to ask/request'); word order places the prepositional 'از من' before the question phrase, yielding 'what should one ask of me [for it]?' i.e. 'what need is there to ask it from me?'.
segments.s263-g163: چنین گفت = 'thus he said' (چنین 'thus/so', گفت here reported-verb 'said'). پیش + پدر = 'in front of / before the father' (پیش used as 'in the presence of'). رزمساز is a descriptive title ('riddle of the compound: one who makes or readies for battle' — here 'war‑maker/armorer'). Word order: statement verb + indication of audience ('پیش پدر رزمساز').
segments.s263-g164: که introduces the content of what was said ('that'). ما را uses را as a topical/ethical object marker ('for us' or 'to us'). به درع تو = 'with/for your armor' (به + درع + possessive تو). ناید نیاز is an idiomatic older construction where ناید (lit. 'does not come/occur') + نیاز ('need') together mean 'there is no need' — so the clause reads 'that for us, with your armor, no need comes' i.e. 'we have no need of your armor.'
