segments.s263-g165: بر آنی که is a compact idiom meaning 'he was intent / bent on the fact that' (بر + آنی; که introduces the subordinate clause). اندر جهان = 'in the world' and سر به سر is a fixed adverbial phrase meaning 'all over / throughout' or 'one and all'; here it qualifies 'in the world' to mean 'throughout the world'. Word order is literary/poetic but follows older Persian idioms.
segments.s263-g166: به درع تو = 'for/after your armor' (به + direct object + possessive). جویند is 3rd person plural present 'they seek' (جویند from جستن/جوییدن). مردان هنر = 'men of skill' (noun phrase where هنر qualifies مردان). In this poetic order the prepositional phrase and verb come before the subject 'مردان هنر'; literally 'to your armor seek (are) men of skill.'
segments.s263-g167: چو = 'if/when' in a conditional sense. درع سیاوش is the noun phrase 'Siavash's armor' (درع + proper name as possessor). نباشد is the negative 3rd-person present/subjunctive of بودن ('is not / be not present') and here means 'is not there/with him.' به جنگ = 'in/at the battle' (prepositional phrase indicating location). The order places the condition first: 'If Siavash's armor is not at battle...'.
segments.s263-g168: نجویند is the negative present plural 'do not seek' (ن + جویند). گردنکشان is a descriptive plural noun 'neck‑breakers' i.e. proud, risk‑taking warriors (subject). نام و ننگ = 'name and shame' but functions idiomatically as 'fame and disgrace' or 'glory and dishonor' — objects of the verb; the clause means 'the proud will not seek (risk) fame or disgrace' i.e. will not risk their necks for glory when the famed armor is absent. Word order places verb before subject, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s263-g201: چنین is an adverb ‘thus/so’ placed before the verb for emphasis. برگزینی is a second‑person singular present (classical Persian form) meaning ‘you choose/select’; همی is the old progressive/emphatic particle (‘do/always’), and رای خویش = ‘your counsel/opinion’ — خوی + ش here forms خویش ‘one’s own’ (possessive). The whole phrase = ‘you thus choose your own counsel.’
segments.s263-g202: ندانی is the second‑person singular negative present of دانستن: ‘you do not know.’ که introduces a subordinate clause ‘that’; چون here functions as ‘how’; آیدت is the impersonal/3rd‑person verb with clitic object ‘to you’ (it comes to you / befalls you); کار = ‘affair/matter’; پیش = ‘before/upon’ — together: ‘you do not know how the matter will come upon you.’
segments.s263-g203: بدو گفت is literal word order ‘to him said’ (classical syntax) meaning ‘he said to him.’ بیژن is the speaker’s name. که‌ای here introduces direct speech and the vocative: que‑ + ای gives the vocative ‘O…’; نیو باب is a compound vocative (ney + bâb) addressed to the addressee — a nickname or epithet derived from the reed‑flute (ney).
segments.s263-g204: دل من = ‘my heart’ (noun + possessive pronoun). ز is the poetic preposition ‘from/because of’; کین = ‘hatred/vengeance’; سیاوش is the name; متاب is the negative imperative (here used as a simple imperative) ‘do not agitate / do not stir’ (classical hortative/imperative form). The clause is a plea: ‘do not stir my heart because of Siavash’s hatred.’
segments.s263-g205: که introduces the explanatory clause ‘that.’ هومان is the proper name subject. نه ... است is the classical negative copula construction (‘is not’). از + روی + وز + آهن here is a somewhat compressed poetic expression: از روی = ‘of/from the nature/appearance of,’ وز = ‘weight/strength,’ آهن = ‘iron’ — together meaning ‘not of iron’s strength’ or ‘not made of iron.’
segments.s263-g206: Continuation of the negation: نه … و نه … است = ‘neither … nor … is.’ پیل ژیان is a poetic pairing meaning something like ‘an elephant in vitality/might’ (پیل = elephant; ژیان = life/vitality). آهرمن (Ahriman) is the devil figure — the speaker claims Homan is neither huge/monster nor demonic. The final است is the copula.
segments.s263-g207: یکی مرد جنگ است = ‘he is a certain man of war’ (یکی as an indefinite/introductory particle ‘a certain’ or ‘one’). و connects the clauses. من جنگجوی = ‘I am a warrior’ (subject omitted before جنگجوی; the pronoun من is explicit here for emphasis). Word order follows classical Persian: predicate after subject with the copula est omitted in the second clause.
segments.s263-g208: از او برنتابم is a negative first‑person present (or hortatory) construction: برنتابم = ‘I will not bear / I will not endure’ with او as its object (preceded by از in classical idiom). به بخت تو = ‘for/by your fortune/fate’ (به + بخت); روی here is a short, emphatic closing (literally ‘face’/‘presence’) used idiomatically to address or invoke — the clause expresses refusal to yield in the matter, with an appeal/reference to the addressee’s fate or honour.
segments.s263-g73: چو دستور باشد sets a conditional ('if/when there is an order'); دستور is the noun 'order/command' and باشد is the present/subjunctive form of 'to be' used here to mark that condition. مرا پهلوان is the object/pronoun (مرا = 'me') plus predicate nominal 'pahlavān' ('champion') anticipating the subjunctive verb in the next segment — together meaning 'let me be champion.'
segments.s263-g74: شوم is the first‑person subjunctive/potential 'may I become' (here a voluntative request). پیش او = 'in the presence of / before him' (پیش = 'in front of / before', او = 'him'). چون + هژبر دمان = a simile 'like Hejbar‑Damān' (چون = 'like/as', هژبر دمان functions as a personal name or epithet).
segments.s263-g75: بفرماید is the narrative/polite present of گفتن/فرمودن used as 'he said' (classical Shahnameh style). اکنون is the adverb 'now/at once'. سپهبد is the title 'spahbed' (the speaker) and به گیو marks the indirect object 'to Giv' (به + proper name).
segments.s263-g76: مگر here functions as an exception/contrast introducer ('except / unless'); کان is an archaic particle that can mean 'as/so' or 'formerly' and here forms an old construction with مگر; سلیح = 'weapon/armament' and سیاووش is the personal name 'Siyâvash'. نیو is likely an adjectival or archaic epithet (here 'new' or a regional form). The exact reading of this short clause is compressed/poetic — grammatically it introduces or qualifies the weapon of Siyâvash.
segments.s263-g77: دهد is the narrative present of 'to give' ('he gives/he gave' in storytelling). مر (مرا) is the object 'to me'. خود here means 'his own' (i.e. his own equipment) and و links to رومی‌زره 'Roman armor/chainmail' as the second item given.
segments.s263-g78: ز is the poetic form of 'from'. بند زره = 'the strap/fastening of the armor' (بند = bond/strap, زره = armor). برگشاید is a past/narrative form 'he loosened/opened' (from برگشودن), and گره is 'knot' — so the clause describes unfastening the knot of the armor.
segments.s263-g79: چو + بشنید is a temporal clause meaning 'when he heard'; بشنید is past tense 'he heard'. گودرز is the proper name (subject) and گفتار اوی = 'his speech/words' (گفتار = speech, اوی = his) is the object of شنیدن.
segments.s263-g80: بدید here is 'he saw' in the narrative past but functions idiomatically as 'he perceived' or 'he observed'. آن دل و رای هشیار اوی = 'that heart and his sensible/alert counsel' (آن = that/the, دل = heart/mind, رای = counsel/plan, هشیار = sensible/awake, اوی = his), describing what he perceived.
segments.s263-g97: بدو (به + او) is the dative/benefactive clitic 'to him/unto him' placed before the verb for poetic word order; گفت is the simple past 'said' with بیژن as subject in an inverted order (object/indirect object + verb + subject). که‌ای پهلوان is a vocative/exclamation ('O champion!') — که here combines with ای to call out to someone.
segments.s263-g98: This clause gives predicates directed at the addressee. هنرمند is an adjective/noun 'skillful, accomplished'; باشد is the optative/jussive 3rd‑person form ('may he be' / 'be'), used here as a hortative/request; دلیر و جوان are coordinate adjectives joined by و ('and'). The sequence is an exhortation: 'be skillful, brave and young.'
segments.s263-g99: مرا‌گر is a conditional phrase ('مرا' = me; 'گر' = if) placed before the verb (poetic inversion). بدیدی is a classical past/conditional form meaning 'you see/were to see' used in the protasis. به رزم is a locative/causal phrase 'in/at battle' and فرود is a noun/past participle 'fall' — together 'if you see me fall in battle.'
segments.s263-g100: ز سر (archaic ordering of از سر) here functions as 'anew / from the head' idiomatically meaning 'again, afresh'; باز reinforces the sense of 'again.' باید is the impersonal/modal 'it is necessary / you must'; کنون is 'now'; آزمود is the past stem used in classical Persian where modern Persian would use an infinitive — here it carries the sense 'to test/try' (i.e., 'you must now test/try again').
segments.s263-g101: به جنگ = 'in battle' (locative phrase). پشن is an archaic/dialectal form of پشت 'back' placed before the verb for emphasis. بر + نوشتم (نوشتم = first‑person past of a poetic/archaic verb) literally 'I wrote on' but here idiomatically means 'I struck/pressed (it) upon' or 'I laid (my back) upon the ground' — a poetic way of saying he will lay his back on the earth in battle.
segments.s263-g102: نبیند is the negative jussive/optative/present form 'let/shall not see' (n‑ + بیند from دیدن); کسی in negative contexts functions as 'anyone' → here effectively 'no one'; پشت من = 'my back'; روز کین = 'the day of vengeance' (کین = vengeance). Word order is verb → quantifier → object (poetic ordering) yielding 'no one will see my back on the day of vengeance.'
segments.s263-g103: Here مـرا (written as م + را in the tokenization) functions as a dative/topic 'for me' (مرا = 'to/for me'). زندگانی 'life' is the subject; نه negates the predicate; اندر خورست is an archaic construction meaning roughly 'is not fit/for (my) consumption / is not for (me)' — idiomatically 'life is not for me' or 'life has no place for me.' Word order is poetic (dative/topic fronted).
segments.s263-g104: گر is the conditional 'if'; از introduces the comparative 'compared with'; دیگرانم ('دیگران' + enclitic '-م') means 'other people (as compared with me)' — the enclitic marks the comparison as applying to the speaker; هنر 'skill/prowess'; کمترست = 'is less' (comparative predicate). Together: 'If, compared with others, my skill is less...' (the consequence is implied).
segments.s263-g57: کز (archaic for از) here means 'from/out of' and introduces the source phrase 'این رزمگه' ('this battlefield'). The nuclear clause places the verb ساختی (past, 2nd person singular) at the end: '[you] made a garden out of this battlefield.' The subject 'تو' is omitted but understood from the verb.
segments.s263-g58: دل is the theme 'the heart'. از + کین ترکان is a prepositional phrase 'from/of the hatred of the Turks' marking what the heart is rid of. بپرداختی is a 2nd-person singular past of پرداختن used poetically to mean 'dispelled/removed' (here 'freed from'); the verb again carries the subject implicitly.
segments.s263-g59: شگفتی‌تر is a comparative adjective 'more astonishing/stranger.' آنک (an archaic particle) introduces the content clause 'that ...' (i.e. 'it is more astonishing that ...'). از میان سپاه is a locative prepositional phrase 'from/among the midst of the army' that sets the scene for the following noun phrase.
segments.s263-g60: یکی ترک is an indefinite noun phrase 'a certain / one Turk.' The adjectives/participles بدبخت (unfortunate) and گم‌کرده (having lost) both post‑modify the noun, and راه ('way') completes the participial phrase: 'a certain unfortunate Turk who had lost his way.'
segments.s263-g61: بیامد که is a fixed narrative turn 'it came to pass that...' introducing the following event. یزدان نیکی‌کنش is a nominative phrase 'God, doing good / in His kindness' (نیکی‌کنش = 'the doing of good' or 'beneficent action'), indicating the agent or source of the subsequent action.
segments.s263-g62: همی is a continuative adverb 'continually/now' placed before the verb. سگالید is an archaic/hunting verb 'to set dogs on / to hunt with hounds'; بد before it qualifies the action as ill/strong (poetic intensifier). با بد تنش is a prepositional phrase 'with his wretched body' where ش is the enclitic 'his' attached to تن. Overall: '[God], by his favour, continually set hounds upon his wretched body.'
segments.s263-g63: بیاوردش is a past-tense perfective verb 'brought him' (subject omitted). از پیش توران سپاه is a locative/genitive chain: از پیش = 'from before/in the presence of', توران سپاه = 'the Turanian host/army' (توران governing سپاه). The phrase as a whole: 'they brought him from before the Turanian army.'
segments.s263-g64: بدان is here a fused/discourse particle meaning 'by that/thereby' (an archaic poetic usage); تا introduces a result/purpose clause 'so that/in order that.' به دست تو is 'into your hand/power' (به + دست, where 'دست' denotes power/control), گردد is a subjunctive/optative form of گشت 'become' indicating the hoped-for/resultant state, and تباه is the predicate 'ruined/destroyed.' Together: 'thereby so that he should come into your hand and be ruined.'
segments.s263-g33: ترا گفته بودم is a preposed object + perfect-periphrasis: ترا (old clitic for 'to you' = تو را) precedes گفته (past participle 'said') and بودم functions as an auxiliary in the pluperfect ('I had said to you'). که introduces the following subordinate clause. تندی مکن is a prohibitive construction: تندی ('haste, rashness') + مکن (negative imperative 'do not do').
segments.s263-g34: This is an admonition with an object and a negative imperative verb. ز here functions as a poetic preposition ('about/with respect to' or 'from') linking گودرز as the target; بر has the sense 'against' (turning toward somebody). مگردان is negative imperative 'do not turn' (م- negative + گردان imperative stem of گردانیدن/گردیدن), and سخن is the object 'speech' placed after the verb for emphasis.
segments.s263-g35: که introduces an explanatory clause ('for' / 'because' / 'that'). او is the third-person pronoun 'he'. کار دیده‌ست is a compound: کار ('the affair / the matter') + دیده‌ست (perfect 'has seen/experienced') — an idiom meaning 'he has experience'. و links that clause to the predicative adjective داناترست, where داناتر- is comparative 'more knowing' plus -است (here fused as -ست) 'is'.
segments.s263-g36: بدین is the classical compound meaning 'in/with this' (modern به این), here marking 'this [army]'; the tokenizer split it into ب + دین, but دین as listed senses does not match this usage (so -1). لشکر نامور is a noun phrase 'renowned army/host' (لشکر 'army', نامور 'renowned'), and مهترست is the copula phrase 'is the chief/elder' (مهتر 'chief/elder' + -ست 'is'). Word order places the demonstrative/locative before the noun, then the predicate.
segments.s263-g37: سواران جنگی is the subject ('warlike riders' / 'mounted warriors') with the adjective following the noun in classical order. به پیش is a locative/directional phrase 'to the front / forward', and اندرند is an archaic present plural copula/verb meaning 'they are (present / stand)'. The order is subject — adverbial phrase — verb.
segments.s263-g38: که introduces a subordinate purpose/result clause ('that / so that'). بر کینه‌گه is a prepositional phrase: بر with the sense 'against / toward' and کینه‌گه 'the place/seat of hatred' (a compound meaning 'lair of enmity'). پیل را marks 'the elephant' with the object marker را. بشکرند is a third-person plural verb form; its exact lexical reading is unclear from the provided senses (the listed senses are unrelated here), so its precise meaning must be inferred from context as 'attack/assail' or a similar action — grammatically it is the finite verb agreeing with the plural subject.
segments.s263-g39: نفرمود is the verb 'فرمود' (he said/ordered) with the negative prefix ن- here (so 'he did not say/command'); the listed sense chosen is the base 'said/command' but note the negation in the actual form. با او 'with him' is the prepositional complement. کسی را uses کسی 'someone' with the object marker را, and نبرد appears as the noun/verb 'fight / battle' agreeing as the action being negated or disallowed — the whole phrase means roughly 'he did not order that anyone fight with him' or 'he did not allow anyone to fight him.'
segments.s263-g40: This is a rhetorical question. جوانی 'a youth / young man' is the topic; مگر introduces a rhetorical contrast/expectation ('was it not / surely'), here functioning as 'was it not that...'. مر ترا is an enclitic order 'to me — you' (مر = me, ت = you) with را marking the object afterward (Tara: 'you to me'). خیره کرد is a causative past: خیره 'staring/astonished' + کرد 'made/caused' (کرد used with the sense 'to cause'), so 'did a youth cause you to stare at me?'
segments.s263-g9: به پیش پدر شد is a compact classical phrase: به (to/toward) + پیش (before/in the presence of) + پدر (father) + شد (past verb). شد here indicates motion/arrival or entry into the state of being before someone (classical use), so the whole means 'he came/approached before his father.' پر از + کیمیا is an adjectival phrase following the verb: پر 'full (of)' governs از + کیمیا ('of kīmiyā'), describing his condition as he approached.
segments.s263-g10: سخن گفت با او is a simple clause: سخن (speech) + گفت (spoke/said) + با او (with him). The clause is followed by ز بهر نیا where ز بهر = 'for/because of' (bihār used classically) and نیا is the beneficiary/aim ('Niyâ' or 'the ancestor' depending on context). Note classical ordering: the purpose phrase (ز بهر ...) comes after the speech phrase.
segments.s263-g11: چنین گفت مر گیو را is inverted narrative word order: چنین گفت ('thus he said') + مر ('to me') + گیو را (Giv marked by را). مر is the dative-style clitic/old form 'to me'; را after a name here marks the named person as the (grammatical) object/recipient in poetry. کای پدر is a vocative: کای = 'O' (classical vocative) + پدر 'father'.
segments.s263-g12: بگفتم ترا من همه در به در is a reported direct speech line. بگفتم is the past first-person 'I said' (بـ- prefix is classical). ترا is the object 'you' split orthographically here into ت (second-person clitic) + را (object marker). من 'I' is emphatic. همه در به در is an idiomatic adverbial phrase meaning 'everywhere / door to door' (در = 'door' used twice with به linking them to mean from door to door).
segments.s263-g13: که introduces an explanatory clause ('that'/'for'). گودرز را uses را as a topical/emphatic marker putting Goudarz in focus. هوش کمتر شده‌ست is a predicative clause: هوش 'intellect/awareness' + کمتر 'less' + شده‌ست (perfect auxiliary) 'has become/has grown', so literally 'Goudarz's awareness has become less.'
segments.s263-g14: به آیین نبینی is a compact idiom: به آیین 'in (his) manner/condition' with به used adverbially, and نبینی is present-subjunctive/negative form of دیدن 'you do not see' (classical usage can function as a general statement 'one does not see / you do not see'). که دیگر شده‌ست completes the thought: که 'that' + دیگر 'different/anymore' + شده‌ست 'has become', i.e. 'you see that he has become different.'
segments.s263-g15: دلش is دل + the possessive suffix ش 'his' (his heart). پر نهیب است is a copular clause: پر 'full (of)' + نهیب (here 'rebuke/clamorous reproach' or a strong inward pang) + است 'is' — 'his heart is full of reproach/anguish.' The coordinate phrase و پرخون جگر links with و 'and' to a similar predicate: پرخون 'bloodied' modifying جگر (literally 'liver' but poetically 'seat of feeling').
segments.s263-g16: ز تیمار و ز درد چندان پسر is a causal/ablative phrase: ز (poetic 'from/because of') + تیمار 'care/solicitude' and ز درد 'and from/of pain' — giving the reason for his state. چندان 'so much/to such an extent' modifies and quantifies the suffering, and پسر 'son' is the object of that solicitude: 'from so much care and pain for (his) son.'
segments.s263-g41: که introduces this subordinate/exclamatory clause ('that' / 'how'); گردن is the noun 'neck/head' placed before the verb for emphasis; بدین سان is a frozen adverbial phrase 'in this manner/so', and برافراختی is the simple past, 2nd person singular of برافراشتن 'you raised/you lifted'. The clause literally reads 'that (you) raised (your) neck in this way.'
segments.s263-g42: بدین here ('with this') combines be- + an adverbial idea; آرزو is the noun 'desire/wish' and functions as the reason/motive; پیش من = 'before me / in my presence / toward me'; تاختی is the past 2nd person of تاختن 'you galloped/charged'. Together: 'with this desire you charged toward me.'
segments.s263-g43: نیم here is used idiomatically to negate or deny identification ('I am not ...'); من is the topical pronoun 'I'; بدین کار = 'in this matter/affair'; همداستان is a noun meaning 'accomplice' or 'conspirator' and is the predicate. The phrase asserts 'I am not an accomplice in this affair.'
segments.s263-g44: مزن is a prohibitive/jussive form meaning 'do not (do/say)'; here it forbids relating the story. نیز = 'also/even'; پیشم = 'before me / to me (in my presence)'; چنین = 'such/like this'; داستان = 'story/tale'. The full sense: 'Do not, even before me, relate such a tale.'
segments.s263-g45: بدو = به + او 'to him' (dative 'to/unto him'); گفت is the verb 'said' (here reported speech) with inverted word order 'to him said Bijan'; بیژن is the speaker's name. که‌گر (که گر) is the conditional conjunction 'if'; کام من = 'my desire/wish' (کام modified by the possessive pronoun).
segments.s263-g46: نجویی is the 2nd-person verbal form of جستن/جوییدن 'you seek/pursue' used in the conditional clause; نخواهی here expresses will/desire (negated in sense by the construction) — together rendered 'if you will not seek/pursue'; مگر introduces the contrast 'except/only/then' and نام من = 'my name/reputation'. The speaker asks rhetorically whether, if the addressee will not pursue his desire, he will at least pursue his name.
segments.s263-g47: شوم is the 1st person subjunctive/optative of شدن 'may I become / let me be', used here as a wish/resolve; پیش سالار = 'in front of / before the commander' (پیش = 'in the presence of'); بسته‌کمر is a compound adjective 'girded at the waist', describing the speaker's readiness for action.
segments.s263-g48: زنم is the 1st person subjunctive/jussive of زدن 'may I strike / I will strike' (here an assertive vow); دست بر جنگ = 'hand upon the battle/war' idiomatically 'to take up arms / to strike in battle'; هومان is the target's name; به بر is a directional/adpositional sequence 'toward / against'. The clause expresses the speaker's vow to attack Homan in combat.
